comments.text: Here is a short proof of the case $|P|=2$, which actually shows that $S$ is transcendental. Let $P =\{p_1<p_2<...<p_k\}$. Then each $a_n$ is of the form $p_1^{i_1}\cdots p_k^{i_k}$, where $i_1,...,i_k\ge0$, and\[\text{lcm}[a_1,...,a_n]=\prod_{j=1}^{k}p_j^{\big\lfloor \frac{\log a_n}{\log p_j}\big\rfloor}.\]Hence\[S=\sum_{i_1,...,i_k\ge0}\prod_{j=1}^{k}p_j^{-\big\lfloor \frac{\log p_1^{i_1}\cdots p_k^{i_k}}{\log p_j}\big\rfloor}.\]Specializing to the case $P=\{p<q\}$ and setting $\beta=\log q/\log p>1$ which is irrational, we have\[S=\sum_{i,j\ge0}p^{-i-\lfloor j\beta\rfloor}q^{-j-\lfloor i\beta^{-1}\rfloor}=S_1S_2,\]where \begin{align*} S_1&:=\sum_{i\ge0}p^{-i}q^{-\lfloor i\beta^{-1}\rfloor},\\ S_2&:=\sum_{j\ge0}p^{-\lfloor j\beta\rfloor}q^{-j}. \end{align*} We can relate $S_1,S_2$ to the Hecke--Mahler series\[F_{\theta}(z_1,z_2):=\sum_{i_1\ge1}z_1^{i_1}\sum_{1\le i_2\le i_1\theta}z_2^{i_2},\]where $|z_1|,|z_2|<1$ and $\theta>0$. When $\theta\ge1$, we have\[F_{\theta}(z_1,z_2)=\frac{z_2}{1-z_2}\left(\frac{z_1}{1-z_1}-\sum_{i_1\ge1}z_1^{i_1}z_2^{\lfloor i_1\theta\rfloor}\right),\]and so\[S_2=\frac{1}{q-1}-(p-1)F_{\beta}(1/q,1/p)+1.\]Moreover, for any $\theta>0$,\[F_{\theta}(z_1,z_2)+F_{\theta^{-1}}(z_2,z_1)=\sum_{i_1,i_2\ge1}z_1^{i_1}z_2^{i_2}=\frac{z_1z_2}{(1-z_1)(1-z_2)}.\]This yields the nice identity\[(p-1)S_1+(q-1)S_2=p+q-1,\]which allows one to write $S$ as a quadratic polynomial with rational coefficients in $S_2$. We know by the classical theory of Hecke--Mahler series that $F_{\beta}(1/q,1/p)$ is transcendental. Therefore, $S_2$ is also transcendental, and so is $S$. This argument does not seem to generalize immediately to $|P|\ge3$, since $S$ does not factor nicely in the first place.

Your argument does not need p and q to be different primes. It also works for arbitrary coprime numbers r and s. EDIT: Perhaps Erdos' question makes sense not only for sets of primes, but for sets of mutually coprime numbers.

Thank you for your comment. I agree with you on this. More generally, one may consider the case where $P$ consists of nonzero algebraic numbers $p_1,...,p_k$, where $k\ge2$, such that the numbers $\log p_1,..,\log p_k$ are linearly independent over $\mathbb Q$. The elements generated by $P$ may be ordered according to their norms.

Here is a proof for the case where $P=\{p_1<p_2<\cdots\}$ is infinite. Denote the series in consideration by $S$. Let $\{q_i\}_{i\ge1}$ be the ascending sequence of the powers of primes in $P$, and let $x_i:=[q_1,...,q_i]$ for $i\ge1$. For each prime power $q=p^k$, define $\alpha(q):=p$. Then $x_{i+1}=\alpha(q_{i+1})x_i$ and $q_{i+1}\le p_1q_{i}$ for all $i\ge1$. Observe that\[S=\sum_{i\ge1} rac{m_i}{x_i},\]where $m_i$ counts the number of $a_n$'s in $[q_i,q_{i+1})$. It is not hard to see that $m_i=o(q_i)$. Indeed, when $\sum_{p
ot\in P} rac{1}{p}=\infty$, Brun's sieve yields\[m_i\ll(q_{i+1}-q_i)\prod_{\substack{p\le q_{i+1}-q_i\p
otin P}}\left(1- rac{1}{p}ight).\]So for sufficiently large $i$, either $q_{i+1}-q_i=o(q_i)$ or the product above is $o(1)$. In both cases we have $m_i=o(q_i)$. When $\sum_{p
ot\in P} rac{1}{p}<\infty$, the Prime Number Theorem with the classical error term gives\[\sum_{\substack{p\le x\p\in P}} rac{1}{p}=\log\log x+C+O\left(e^{-c_0\sqrt{\log x}}ight)\]with some absolute constants $C$ and $c_0>0$, which implies particularly that given any $\epsilon>0$, every interval $(x,(1+\epsilon)x]$ with $x$ sufficiently large contains a prime in $P$, so that $m_i\le q_{i+1}-q_i\le\epsilon q_i$. Assume now that $S= rac{a}{b}$, where $a,b\in\mathbb{N}$. Suppose that $q_{N+1}\in P$ . Then\[bx_N\sum_{i\ge N+1} rac{m_i}{x_i}=ax_N-bx_N\sum_{i\le N} rac{m_i}{x_i}\in\mathbb{N}.\]Since $q_i\le p_1^{i-N-1}q_{N+1}=p_1^{i-N-1}\alpha(q_{N+1})$, we have\[bx_N\sum_{i\ge N+1} rac{m_i}{x_i} =o\left(\sum_{i\ge N+1} rac{q_i}{\alpha(q_{N+1})\cdots \alpha(q_{i})}ight)=o\left(\sum_{i\ge N+1} rac{p_1^{i-N-1}}{\alpha(q_{N+2})\cdots \alpha(q_{i})}ight)\]for sufficiently large $N$. Note that\[\sum_{i\ge N+1} rac{p_1^{i-N-1}}{\alpha(q_{N+2})\cdots \alpha(q_{i})}\le\sum_{i\ge N+1}\left( rac{p_1}{p_2}ight)^{n_i}=\sum_{k\ge0}\left( rac{p_1}{p_2}ight)^{k}\sum_{\substack{i\ge N+1\n_i=k}}1,\]where $n_i:=\#\{j\in[N+2,i]\colon\alpha(q_j)>p_1\}$. Since each interval $(p_1^{r},p_1^{r+l}]$ with $l=\lceil rac{\log p_2}{\log p_1}ceil$ contains a power of $p_2$, we have\[\sum_{\substack{i\ge N+1\n_i=k}}1\le l+1,\]so that\[\sum_{k\ge0}\left( rac{p_1}{p_2}ight)^{k}\sum_{\substack{i\ge N+1\n_i=k}}1\le(l+1)\sum_{k\ge0}\left( rac{p_1}{p_2}ight)^{k}\ll1.\]So\[bx_N\sum_{i\ge N+1} rac{m_i}{x_i} =o(1)\]for sufficiently large $N$, a contradiction.

(EDIT: This comment was in reply to an earlier version of Steve's comment, which has since been edited.) It's not true that $x_{n+1}\geq 2x_n$ for all $n$ - for example, this certainly fails when $P$ is the set of all primes. Your proof can be amended to do without this assumption though, just showing convergence of the final tail series more directly.
Thank you very much for pointing this out. I guess I was a bit careless. I will fix it soon.

Erdos conjectured these are always irrational. One is inclined to these general conclusions under the guise of so called meta conjectures, Which assert that series are irrational unless there is a clear reason they should not be.
